does: JailPO introduces a black-box jailbreak framework that automatically generates adversarial prompts to bypass safety measures in aligned large language models. The approach trains attack models to produce covert jailbreak prompts without requiring access to model internals or gradients. The framework incorporates preference optimization techniques to enhance attack effectiveness, where an attacker-controlled preference model guides the generation of prompts that maximize the likelihood of harmful outputs. The paper evaluates the method across three distinct jailbreak patterns and demonstrates that JailPO achieves high attack success rates while maintaining automation, scalability, and universality compared to existing template-based and gradient-dependent optimization approaches.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining vulnerabilities in RLHF-aligned language models, extending prior jailbreak studies that relied on handcrafted prompts or white-box optimization techniques. It responds to limitations in existing attack methods by adopting preference optimization, a technique increasingly used to enhance model behaviors through learned reward signals. The framework connects to research on adversarial attacks in NLP and reinforcement learning from human feedback, particularly work exploring how aligned models can still be manipulated despite safety training. JailPO also relates to broader security research investigating robustness of neural networks and the gap between training objectives and actual deployment safety, building on insights that alignment techniques may not fully eliminate harmful capabilities.
enables: This work enables deeper investigation into the robustness of RLHF-aligned language models and could guide the development of more effective defense mechanisms. By demonstrating automated and scalable jailbreak techniques, it creates a foundation for red-teaming large language models systematically. The preference optimization attack approach may inspire future research into adversarial training methods that specifically defend against learned attack strategies. The framework's flexibility in supporting multiple jailbreak patterns suggests avenues for understanding diverse failure modes of alignment. Furthermore, this research could motivate the development of stronger alignment techniques, evaluation protocols for assessing model resilience, and new theoretical understanding of why aligned models remain vulnerable to prompt manipulation despite safety training.
